hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: Advanced Modals -- Would Rather, Had Better, May As Well
lesson.menu_desc: Ana's remote team must choose a testing tool for a new module. She writes a recommendation: her preference (I would rather use the open-source tool), her warning (we had better decide this week), and her pragmatic call (we are blocked, so we may as well set it up now) -- using advanced modals to recommend and prefer -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- Would Rather, Had Better, May As Well
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 14 -- Advanced Modals
lesson.grammar_point: advanced modals (would rather, had better, may as well)

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a thing you like or want more than another
to advise something as the best choice
worth the time, money or effort
to choose one option instead of others
realistic and able to work as a choice
the positive part or advantage of a choice

paras: Ana's remote team had to choose a testing tool for a new module, and two options were viable. One was a paid tool with support; the other was open-source and free. Ana had a clear preference, so she wrote a short recommendation for the team with her rationale. 'I would rather use the open-source tool than the paid one,' she wrote, and she explained why. The upside was that it was free and the whole team already knew it, so setup would be fast. She did not just say what she liked; she made a compelling case, because a good recommendation needs reasons, not only opinions.

There was a deadline, though, and some teammates were reluctant to switch tools at all. Ana knew that waiting was the real danger, so she gave the team firm advice: 'We had better decide this week, or we will run out of time before the release.' She was leaning toward the open-source option, but she stayed honest about the downside: it had less official support. Even so, she thought it was advisable to start early rather than wait for a perfect answer. It was more prudent to pick a good-enough tool now than a perfect one too late.

One more thing helped Ana make the call. The team was already blocked, waiting on another team's API, so nobody could do their main task that afternoon. 'We are blocked anyway,' Ana wrote, 'so we may as well set up the new tool now and test it while we wait.' It was a worthwhile use of dead time. In the end, the team read her rationale, agreed it was a viable and low-risk plan, and opted for her recommendation. Ana did not force anyone; she recommended, she preferred, and she gave a reason for every choice.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B2 reading, tool selection and technical recommendations

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Ana forces her team to use the paid tool because she does not trust free software.
Ana makes a clear, reasoned recommendation: she states her preference, warns the team to decide soon, and uses dead time to set the tool up.
The text explains how to write automated tests from zero.

items: Ana would rather use the open-source tool than the paid one, and she gives reasons for her preference.

gapfill.text: Notice three advanced modals for recommending and preferring. WOULD RATHER + base verb = preference: I would rather use the free tool. HAD BETTER + base verb = strong advice or warning: We had better decide this week. MAY AS WELL + base verb = there is no better option, so why not: We are blocked, so we may as well set it up now.

bank.label: Useful sentences for recommending and preferring
items: I would rather use the open-source tool than the paid one.
We had better decide this week, or we will run out of time.
We are blocked anyway, so we may as well set it up now.
My recommendation is the free tool, and here is my rationale.
It is advisable to start early rather than wait for a perfect answer.
The upside is that the whole team already knows the tool.
I am leaning toward the open-source option, but I am honest about the downside.
It is a viable, low-risk plan, so I would opt for it.

lf.title: Recommend it with the right modal
items: A
I
would rather
use the free tool than the paid one. (preference)
strong
B
We
had better
decide this week, or we will miss the deadline. (strong advice / warning)
strong
C
We are blocked anyway, so we
may as well
set up the tool now. (no better option)
strong
D
It is
advisable
to start early rather than wait. (recommendation)
soft

lf.text: Three modals, three jobs. WOULD RATHER + base verb (than...) = what you prefer. HAD BETTER + base verb = firm advice, almost a warning. MAY AS WELL + base verb = it makes no difference, so you might as well. All three take the base verb with no 'to': would rather USE, had better DECIDE, may as well SET UP.

items.situation: Your team must pick between a paid tool and a free one you all know. State your preference with 'would rather'.
tips: I would rather use the free tool than the paid one, because we already know it.
I would rather opt for the open-source option, since setup would be faster.

items.situation: The deadline is close and nobody has decided. Give the team firm advice with 'had better'.
tips: We had better decide this week, or we will run out of time.
We had better not wait any longer, or we will miss the release.

items.situation: The team is blocked, waiting on another team's API. Suggest a good use of the dead time with 'may as well'.
tips: We are blocked anyway, so we may as well set up the new tool now.
Since we cannot do our main task, we may as well test the tool while we wait.

items.situation: Explain the upside of your recommended tool and why the plan is worthwhile.
tips: The upside is that the whole team already knows it, so it is a worthwhile choice.
It is a viable, low-risk plan, and the upside is a much faster setup.

items: Round 1 -- Guided
Recommend one of two tools to your team. State your preference with 'would rather' and give one reason (the upside). Speak from your notes.
Round 2 -- Add the warning
Now add firm advice with 'had better': tell the team why they should decide soon, and be honest about one downside.
Round 3 -- The pragmatic call
Your team is blocked. Give your whole recommendation with no notes: your preference, your warning, and a 'may as well' suggestion to use the dead time.
Curveball
A teammate says, 'Let's just wait until next sprint to decide.' Answer with 'had better' and explain why waiting is not advisable.

answerkey.title: Reveal the recommendation toolkit
list: Vocabulary: preference, to recommend, advisable, worthwhile, to opt for, viable, upside, rationale, reluctant, to lean toward, prudent, compelling.
State a preference: 'I would rather use the free tool than the paid one.'
Give firm advice: 'We had better decide this week, or we will run out of time.'
Make a pragmatic call: 'We are blocked anyway, so we may as well set it up now.'
Support it: give the upside, the rationale, and one honest downside. Structure: prefer, advise, and use dead time well -- every choice with a reason.
answerkey.note: A good recommendation is not just an opinion. State your preference, give firm advice, and back every choice with a reason.

listenings.text: Hi Ana, this is David from the platform team. I read your tooling recommendation, and I think it is really clear. I have two questions before I share it with the others. First, you said you would rather use the open-source tool than the paid one. Is that mostly about the cost, or is it also about speed of setup? I want to give the right reason when people ask me. Second, you wrote that we had better decide this week. Is that a hard deadline, or is there a little room if we need one more day? I am not pushing back, I just want to be honest with the team. Oh, and I liked the 'we are blocked anyway, so we may as well set it up now' idea. That is a smart use of the dead time. Send me two short lines and I will forward your recommendation. Thanks, Ana.

Hi Ana, it is Priya, your team lead. I want to give you a quick tip on writing recommendations, because yours are getting really strong. The secret is to separate three things: what you prefer, what you advise, and what is just practical. When you say 'I would rather', you are sharing a preference, and that is fine, but always add the reason, the upside, so it is not only an opinion. When you say 'we had better', you are giving firm advice, almost a warning, so use it for the things that really matter, like a deadline. And 'may as well' is my favorite: it turns dead time into progress. We are blocked, so we may as well set up the tool. One more thing: be honest about the downside. A recommendation that hides the risk is not compelling, it is just optimistic. State the upside and the downside, and let the team decide. That is what makes your rationale so easy to trust.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana built her tooling recommendation. First, she compared the two viable tools and formed a clear preference for the open-source one. Then she wrote her rationale, explaining the upside and making a compelling case. After that, she warned the team that they had better decide this week, before the deadline. Next, she was honest about the downside, the weaker support, so the team could trust her. Finally, since the team was blocked, she suggested they may as well set up the tool now and use the dead time well.
